items.back: Minimum I_Z: lowest V_in combined with maximum load current. Maximum I_Z: highest V_in combined with no load (all of I_R flows through the Zener). Design R_s for the minimum case; check power for the maximum case.
items.hint: Worst case for staying in regulation vs worst case for power.

items.front: Why does a bare Zener make a poor precision voltage reference?
items.back: Its breakdown voltage shifts with temperature (tempco) and with current through the dynamic resistance r_z, and the regulator wastes power in R_s. Precision designs use bandgap or buried-Zener IC references for low drift and noise.
items.hint: Tempco, r_z, efficiency.

items.front: How does Zener dynamic resistance r_z affect line/load regulation?
items.back: The output voltage changes by about delta V_o ~ r_z * delta I_Z for any change in current. A smaller r_z (better/larger Zeners) gives tighter regulation. It also sets the divider ratio for ripple rejection from the input.
items.hint: delta V = r_z times delta I.

items.prompt: A 5.1 V Zener is fed from 12 V through R_s = 220 ohm with no load. Roughly what power does the Zener dissipate?
items.explanation: I_R = (12 - 5.1)/220 = 6.9/220 ~ 31.4 mA. With no load all of it flows through the Zener, so P_Z = V_Z * I_Z = 5.1 V * 31.4 mA ~ 160 mW. Pick a Zener rated above this.

items.prompt: A shunt Zener regulator drops out of regulation when the load is heaviest. The most likely fix is:
choices: Decrease R_s so more current is available
items.explanation: Heavy load steals current from the Zener; if I_Z falls below I_Zmin the diode leaves breakdown and the output sags. Lowering R_s raises I_R so enough current remains for the Zener at full load (at the cost of more dissipation at light load).

items.prompt: Which Zener voltage gives the lowest temperature coefficient (most stable reference)?
items.explanation: Around 5-6 V the negative tempco of Zener tunneling and the positive tempco of avalanche roughly cancel, giving near-zero temperature coefficient. This is why ~5.6 V references are common.

lessons.title: Rectifiers, Ripple & Filter Capacitors
lessons.content:
Rectifiers turn AC into DC by routing current one way. Three classic topologies:

- **Half-wave**: one diode passes one polarity. Output ripple frequency = line frequency; poor utilization; PIV $= V_p$.
- **Full-wave center-tapped**: two diodes and a tapped transformer conduct on alternate halves. Ripple frequency $= 2f_{line}$; each diode sees PIV $= 2V_p$.
- **Full-wave bridge**: four diodes, no center tap. Ripple $= 2f_{line}$; PIV $= V_p$ per diode, but **two** diode drops are in the path (~1.4 V loss).

A **filter capacitor** across the load smooths the pulsing DC: the cap charges to the peak, then discharges into the load between peaks. For light ripple the cap recharges briefly each cycle, so:

$$V_{ripple(pp)} \\approx \\frac{I_{load}}{f \\, C}$$

where $f$ is the ripple frequency ($f_{line}$ for half-wave, $2f_{line}$ for full-wave). Rearranged to **size the cap** for a target ripple:

$$C \\ge \\frac{I_{load}}{f \\, V_{ripple(pp)}}$$

The DC output sits roughly at $V_p$ minus diode drops minus half the ripple. The full-wave's doubled ripple frequency **halves** the required capacitance versus half-wave — a real reason bridges dominate. Watch the trade-offs: bigger $C$ means lower ripple but **higher, narrower** peak charging-current pulses that stress the diodes and transformer and worsen power factor. **Peak Inverse Voltage** drives diode selection; derate generously. This unregulated rail then feeds a regulator (Module 12).

items.front: Compare the ripple frequency of half-wave vs full-wave rectifiers on a 60 Hz line.
items.back: Half-wave output ripples at the line frequency (60 Hz). Both full-wave types (center-tapped and bridge) ripple at twice the line frequency (120 Hz), because they use both half-cycles. Higher ripple frequency means less filtering needed.
items.hint: Full-wave uses both halves => 2x.

items.front: Give the approximate peak-to-peak ripple voltage for a capacitor-filtered rectifier.
items.back: V_ripple(pp) ~ I_load/(f*C), where I_load is the DC load current, f is the ripple frequency (f_line for half-wave, 2*f_line for full-wave), and C is the filter capacitance. It assumes the cap discharges roughly linearly between peaks.
items.hint: I over f times C.

items.front: What is Peak Inverse Voltage (PIV), and how does it differ for bridge vs center-tapped full-wave?
items.back: PIV is the maximum reverse voltage a diode must withstand when off. In a bridge each diode sees PIV ~ V_p. In a center-tapped full-wave each diode sees PIV ~ 2*V_p (it blocks across the whole secondary). Select and derate diodes accordingly.
items.hint: Bridge ~ Vp, center-tap ~ 2Vp.

items.front: How many diode drops are in the conduction path of a full-wave bridge, and why does it matter?
items.back: Two diodes conduct in series each half-cycle, so ~1.4 V (2 x 0.7 V) is lost. This matters for low-voltage rails and efficiency; a center-tapped rectifier or a Schottky bridge reduces the loss.
items.hint: Two diodes in series each half-cycle.

items.front: What is the downside of making the filter capacitor very large?
items.back: Lower ripple, but the cap recharges in a shorter window each cycle, producing tall, narrow peak current pulses that stress the diodes and transformer (high I^2R heating) and degrade power factor. There is a ripple-vs-peak-current trade-off.
items.hint: Narrow, tall charging-current spikes.

items.prompt: A full-wave bridge on a 60 Hz line supplies 0.5 A with a 4700 uF filter cap. Approximate peak-to-peak ripple using V ~ I/(fC)?
items.explanation: Full-wave ripple frequency = 120 Hz. V_ripple = I/(f*C) = 0.5/(120 * 4700e-6) = 0.5/0.564 ~ 0.89 V.

items.prompt: For the same load current and ripple spec, how does the required filter cap for a full-wave rectifier compare to a half-wave one?
items.explanation: C >= I/(f*V_ripple). Full-wave doubles the ripple frequency f, so for the same I and V_ripple the required C is halved. This is a key practical advantage of full-wave rectification.

items.prompt: A center-tapped full-wave rectifier produces a 25 V peak per half-secondary. What PIV rating should each diode have (with margin)?
items.explanation: In a center-tapped full-wave the off diode sees ~2*V_p = 50 V across it. You then derate above that (e.g. a 100 V diode). A bridge would only need ~V_p per diode for the same secondary peak.

items.front: Full-wave bridge rectifier with smoothing capacitor
items.back: Four-diode bridge fed by an AC source, with a smoothing capacitor across the load resistor producing a + and - DC rail.

lessons.title: Clippers, Clamps & Special Diodes
lessons.content:
Beyond rectifying, diodes **shape** and **protect** signals.

**Clippers (limiters)** clamp a waveform's excursion. A diode (optionally with a bias voltage) conducts above a threshold and holds the output near $V_{bias} + 0.7\\text{ V}$, slicing off the peak. Series and shunt forms, single- or double-ended, set positive and/or negative limits. Antiparallel diodes to the rails are the classic **ESD/input-protection** clamp.

**Clamps (DC restorers)** shift a waveform's DC level without changing its shape. A series capacitor charges through a diode to a peak, so the AC rides on a new DC offset; the output peak is pinned at the clamp reference. This recovers DC content lost through AC coupling.

**Voltage multipliers** (Cockcroft-Walton ladders) stack diode-capacitor stages to reach $2V_p$, $3V_p$, ... from a small AC source — handy for high-voltage, low-current rails.

**Special diodes** worth instinct:
- **Schottky**: metal-semiconductor, low drop (~$0.3\\text{ V}$) and **no minority-carrier storage**, so very fast — RF detectors, switching, OR-ing, freewheeling.
- **Varactor (varicap)**: reverse-biased junction whose depletion capacitance varies with voltage, $C(V) \\propto (V_R + V_{bi})^{-m}$ — tunes VCOs and front-ends (your radar LO world).
- **PIN**: an intrinsic layer makes it a current-controlled RF resistor/switch and attenuator at microwave.
- **LED / photodiode**: light out / current in; **tunnel** and **Gunn** diodes exploit negative resistance for microwave oscillators.

The through-line: choose the diode by the parasitic that matters — **drop**, **speed (reverse recovery)**, **capacitance**, or **breakdown** — not just 'a diode.'

items.front: What does a clipper (limiter) circuit do, and how is its threshold set?
items.back: It limits a signal's amplitude excursion by conducting once the input exceeds a level, holding the output near that level. The threshold is set by a bias/reference voltage plus the diode drop (~V_bias + 0.7 V). It can clip the positive side, negative side, or both.
items.hint: Slices off peaks above a bias + 0.7 V.

items.front: What does a clamp (DC restorer) do, and what component stores the offset?
items.back: It shifts the DC level of a waveform without altering its shape, pinning one peak to a reference. A series capacitor charges (through the diode) to set the offset, so the AC waveform rides on the new DC level. Used to restore DC lost through AC coupling.
items.hint: Cap holds the DC shift; shape unchanged.

items.front: Why is a Schottky diode faster than a PN-junction diode?
items.back: A Schottky uses a metal-semiconductor junction with essentially no minority-carrier injection/storage, so it has negligible reverse-recovery time. It also has a lower forward drop (~0.3 V). Ideal for high-frequency rectification, RF detection, OR-ing, and freewheeling.
items.hint: No minority-carrier storage => fast.

items.front: How does a varactor diode work and where is it used?
items.back: Reverse-biased, its depletion-region width (and thus junction capacitance) changes with applied voltage: C(V) ~ (V_R + V_bi)^(-m). This voltage-controlled capacitance tunes VCOs, PLLs, and RF front-end resonant circuits.
items.hint: Voltage-variable depletion capacitance.

items.front: What is a PIN diode and its key RF use?
items.back: A diode with an intrinsic (I) layer between P and N regions. At RF it behaves as a current-controlled resistor (its resistance set by DC bias), making it a linear RF switch, attenuator, or limiter at high frequencies.
items.hint: Intrinsic layer => current-controlled RF resistor.

items.prompt: Antiparallel diodes from a signal input to the supply rails are most commonly used for:
items.explanation: Clamp diodes to the rails conduct when the input swings above V+ or below ground/V-, shunting ESD and overvoltage transients away from the sensitive node. This is a standard input-protection clamp.

items.prompt: A voltage multiplier (diode-capacitor ladder) is the right choice when you need:
choices: High voltage at low current from a small AC source
items.explanation: Cockcroft-Walton style multipliers stack stages to produce 2Vp, 3Vp, ... but their output impedance is high, so they suit high-voltage, low-current loads (e.g. CRT/photomultiplier supplies), not high-current rails.

items.prompt: You need a rectifier in a 1 MHz switching converter where reverse-recovery losses matter most. Best pick?
items.explanation: Schottky diodes have negligible reverse-recovery charge and low forward drop, minimizing switching loss at high frequency. Standard PN diodes suffer reverse-recovery loss; Zeners and varactors are not rectifiers for this job.

items.prompt: A clamp circuit's output waveform compared to its input:
choices: Has the same shape but a shifted DC level
items.explanation: A clamp (DC restorer) preserves the waveform shape and peak-to-peak amplitude but adds/removes a DC offset so one peak sits at a reference. Slicing peaks is a clipper; rectifying is a rectifier; multiplying is a multiplier.

lessons.title: Structure, Regions, and Operation
lessons.content:
A BJT is a three-terminal device built from two back-to-back junctions: **emitter** (heavily doped), a thin lightly-doped **base**, and a **collector** (large area). An NPN stacks n-p-n; a PNP is the polarity mirror. Transistor action depends on the base being thin enough that carriers injected from the emitter diffuse across it and are swept into the collector before they recombine.

The device lives in one of three regions, set by how the two junctions are biased:

- **Cutoff** — both junctions reverse-biased (or $V_{BE}$ below the turn-on knee, ~0.7 V for Si). Essentially no current; the device is an open switch.
- **Forward active** — base-emitter forward biased, base-collector reverse biased. This is the amplifying region: $I_C = \beta I_B$ and $I_C = I_S e^{V_{BE}/V_T}$.
- **Saturation** — both junctions forward biased. $V_{CE}$ collapses to $V_{CE(sat)} \approx 0.1$–$0.2$ V; the device is a closed switch and $I_C < \beta I_B$.

The core current relations: $I_E = I_C + I_B$, $I_C = \beta I_B$, and $\alpha = \frac{I_C}{I_E} = \frac{\beta}{\beta + 1}$ (so $\alpha$ is just under 1, e.g. 0.99 for $\beta = 100$).

In active mode the collector behaves like a current source controlled by $V_{BE}$ (or $I_B$), nearly independent of $V_{CE}$ — that flat output region is what makes the BJT an amplifier. The slight upward slope of $I_C$ with $V_{CE}$ is the **Early effect**, modeled by a finite output resistance $r_o = V_A / I_C$, where $V_A$ is the Early voltage.

items.front: Name the three operating regions of a BJT and the junction bias conditions for each.
items.back: Cutoff: both junctions reverse-biased (no current, open switch). Forward active: base-emitter forward, base-collector reverse (amplifier). Saturation: both junctions forward (closed switch, V_CE collapses).
items.hint: It is set by whether each junction is forward or reverse biased.

items.front: Write the relationship between \(I_C\), \(I_B\), and \(\beta\), and between \(I_E\), \(I_C\), \(I_B\).
items.back: I_C = \beta I_B and I_E = I_C + I_B. Combining, I_E = (\beta + 1) I_B. \beta (also h_FE) typically ranges ~50-300 and varies with device, temperature, and I_C.
items.hint: Emitter carries the sum; collector is beta times base.

items.front: Define \(\alpha\) and relate it to \(\beta\).
items.back: \alpha = I_C / I_E = \beta / (\beta + 1), the fraction of emitter current reaching the collector. It is just below 1 (e.g. 0.99 for \beta = 100). Inversely, \beta = \alpha / (1 - \alpha).
items.hint: It is collector current as a fraction of emitter current.

items.front: What is the Early effect, and how is it modeled?
items.back: As V_CE rises, the reverse-biased collector-base depletion region widens, narrowing the effective base. I_C therefore rises slightly with V_CE instead of being perfectly flat. It is modeled by a finite output resistance r_o = V_A / I_C, where V_A is the Early voltage (extrapolated x-intercept of the output curves).
items.hint: Base-width modulation; finite slope of the I_C-V_CE curves.

items.prompt: An NPN has V_BE = 0.7 V and V_CE = 0.15 V. Which region is it in?
items.explanation: V_BE = 0.7 V forward-biases the base-emitter junction. V_CE = 0.15 V is below V_BE, which means the base-collector junction is also forward biased (V_BC = V_BE - V_CE > 0). Both junctions forward = saturation, with V_CE collapsed to V_CE(sat).

items.prompt: A BJT with \(\beta = 100\) is in active mode with I_B = 20 uA. What is I_C?
items.explanation: I_C = \beta I_B = 100 * 20 uA = 2000 uA = 2 mA. (I_E would be (\beta+1) I_B = 101 * 20 uA = 2.02 mA.)

items.prompt: Why is the BJT base made thin and lightly doped?
choices: So injected carriers diffuse across and reach the collector before recombining, giving high \(\beta\)
To make I_B larger than I_C
items.explanation: A thin, lightly-doped base minimizes recombination of the carriers injected from the heavily-doped emitter, so almost all of them are collected. Few recombine (small I_B), many are collected (large I_C), giving a large current gain \beta = I_C / I_B.

items.prompt: In forward active mode, the collector behaves most like:
choices: A current source controlled by V_BE, nearly independent of V_CE
A fixed resistor to the emitter
items.explanation: In active mode I_C = I_S e^(V_BE/V_T) is set by V_BE (or equivalently I_B) and is almost flat versus V_CE (small slope = Early effect). That voltage-controlled current-source behavior is exactly what makes the BJT an amplifier.

items.front: As a switch, what defines the BJT's two states and a key non-ideality of each?
items.back: OFF = cutoff: V_BE below knee, ~no I_C, but there is small leakage I_CEO. ON = saturation: drive enough I_B (I_B > I_C/\beta, often 'overdrive' by 2-10x) so V_CE drops to V_CE(sat) ~0.1-0.2 V; the non-ideality is the residual saturation voltage (power loss) and slow turn-off from stored base charge.
items.hint: Cutoff vs saturation; leakage and V_CE(sat)/storage time.

lessons.title: DC Biasing, Q-Point, and the Load Line
lessons.content:
Biasing sets the quiescent operating point (**Q-point**: $I_{CQ}$, $V_{CEQ}$) so the transistor sits in the middle of forward active, leaving room to swing without clipping. The enemy is $\beta$: it varies 3:1 between parts and drifts with temperature, so a good bias network makes the Q-point nearly independent of $\beta$.

**Fixed bias** (one base resistor to $V_{CC}$) sets $I_B = (V_{CC} - V_{BE})/R_B$, so $I_C = \beta I_B$ moves directly with $\beta$ — unstable. **Emitter degeneration** adds $R_E$, which provides negative feedback: if $I_C$ rises, the emitter voltage rises, reducing $V_{BE}$ and pushing $I_C$ back down.

The workhorse is **voltage-divider bias**: $R_1$ and $R_2$ set a stiff base voltage, and $R_E$ stabilizes the current. If the divider is stiff ($I_{divider} \gg I_B$), then

$$V_B \approx V_{CC}\frac{R_2}{R_1+R_2}, \qquad I_C \approx I_E = \frac{V_B - V_{BE}}{R_E}.$$

Notice $\beta$ has dropped out — the Q-point is set by resistor ratios, not the transistor. A common rule of thumb is to make the divider current about $10 I_B$ and drop ~1 V (or $V_{CC}/10$) across $R_E$.

The **DC load line** is KVL around the output loop: $V_{CC} = I_C R_C + V_{CE} + I_E R_E$, a straight line on the $I_C$–$V_{CE}$ plane from $(V_{CC}, 0)$ to $(0, V_{CC}/(R_C+R_E))$. The Q-point is where this line crosses the device's $I_B$ curve. For maximum symmetric swing, place $V_{CEQ}$ near the middle of the line. (The AC load line — slope set by $R_C \parallel R_L$ once the bypass cap shorts $R_E$ — is steeper and governs the actual signal swing.)

items.front: Voltage-divider-biased CE amplifier
items.back: R1/R2 set a stiff base voltage; RE stabilizes I_C against beta; RC develops the output voltage. Cin couples the AC input.

items.front: DC load line with Q-point
items.back: The load line runs from (VCC,0) to (0, VCC/(RC+RE)); the Q-point is its intersection with the selected I_B curve. Center VCEQ for maximum symmetric swing.

items.front: Why is voltage-divider bias preferred over fixed (base-resistor) bias?
items.back: It makes the Q-point nearly independent of \beta. A stiff R1/R2 divider sets V_B, and R_E sets I_C \approx (V_B - V_BE)/R_E by ratios of resistors. Fixed bias sets I_B and lets I_C = \beta I_B track \beta, which varies part-to-part and with temperature.
items.hint: Stable Q-point vs a Q-point that follows beta.

items.front: How does the emitter resistor \(R_E\) stabilize the DC operating point?
items.back: It is series (current) negative feedback. If I_C rises (e.g. \beta or temperature up), the emitter voltage I_E*R_E rises, which reduces V_BE = V_B - V_E, which reduces I_C back toward its original value. The base voltage V_B is held by the stiff divider, so R_E sets the current.
items.hint: Rising current raises V_E, cuts V_BE, opposes the change.

items.front: Write the load-line equation for a CE stage and give its two endpoints.
items.back: KVL: V_CC = I_C*R_C + V_CE + I_E*R_E, so (with I_E \approx I_C) V_CE = V_CC - I_C(R_C + R_E). Endpoints: cutoff at (V_CE = V_CC, I_C = 0) and saturation at (V_CE \approx 0, I_C = V_CC/(R_C + R_E)).
items.hint: A straight line from VCC on the x-axis to VCC/(RC+RE) on the y-axis.

items.prompt: Voltage-divider bias: V_CC = 12 V, R1 = 47k, R2 = 10k, R_E = 1k, V_BE = 0.7 V. Estimate I_CQ (stiff-divider approximation).
items.explanation: V_B = 12 * 10k/(47k+10k) = 12 * 0.175 = 2.1 V. I_C \approx I_E = (V_B - V_BE)/R_E = (2.1 - 0.7)/1000 = 1.4 mA. Note beta never entered the calculation.

items.prompt: For maximum symmetric output voltage swing in a CE stage, the Q-point V_CEQ should be placed:
choices: Near the middle of the load line
As close to saturation as possible
As close to cutoff as possible
items.explanation: Centering V_CEQ leaves equal headroom toward cutoff (output swinging up to V_CC) and toward saturation (output swinging down to V_CE(sat)). Placing it near either rail clips that half of the signal first. (Strictly, center on the AC load line for the real signal swing.)

items.prompt: A common design guideline is to set the divider (bleeder) current through R1/R2 to roughly:
choices: Equal to the collector current I_C
items.explanation: Making I_divider ~10*I_B keeps the divider 'stiff' so I_B drawn from the node does not appreciably shift V_B, validating V_B \approx V_CC*R2/(R1+R2). Much higher wastes power and lowers Z_in; much lower makes V_B beta-dependent again.

items.prompt: What distinguishes the AC load line from the DC load line in a bypassed CE stage?
choices: The AC load line is steeper because the bypassed R_E disappears and R_L appears in parallel with R_C
The AC load line is flatter because R_E is added
The AC load line ignores the Q-point
items.explanation: At signal frequencies the emitter bypass cap shorts R_E, and the load R_L appears in parallel with R_C. The effective AC load resistance R_C||R_L is smaller than R_C+R_E, so the AC load line through the Q-point has a steeper slope and sets the true achievable swing.

lessons.title: Small-Signal Model and Transconductance
lessons.content:
Once the Q-point is fixed, signals ride on top of it as small perturbations, and we linearize the device about that point. The central parameter is **transconductance**:

$$g_m = \frac{\partial I_C}{\partial V_{BE}} = \frac{I_C}{V_T},$$

where $V_T = kT/q \approx 25$–$26$ mV at room temperature. So $g_m$ scales directly with bias current — at $I_C = 1$ mA, $g_m \approx 40$ mS. This is the single most useful number in BJT analog design.

The **hybrid-$\pi$ model** represents the active transistor as: a base-emitter resistance $r_\pi = \beta / g_m = \beta V_T / I_C$, a dependent current source $g_m v_{be}$, and an output resistance $r_o = V_A / I_C$ (the Early effect). Equivalently the **T-model** uses the emitter resistance $r_e = V_T / I_E \approx 1/g_m$, with $r_\pi = (\beta + 1) r_e$.

Key consequences:

- The intrinsic voltage gain from base to collector is $-g_m R_C$ (the minus sign = inversion), with an upper bound (intrinsic gain) of $g_m r_o = V_A / V_T$.
- The input resistance looking into the base is $r_\pi$; looking into the emitter it is $r_e$.
- Emitter degeneration $R_E$ that is *not* bypassed reduces gain to $\approx -R_C / (r_e + R_E)$ but linearizes the stage and stabilizes gain against $g_m$ variation.

At high frequency the model adds the junction capacitances $C_\pi$ (base-emitter) and $C_\mu$ (base-collector). $C_\mu$ bridges input and output and is the seed of the **Miller effect**, which dominates the bandwidth of the common-emitter stage (next lesson). For hand analysis, remember $g_m = I_C/V_T$ and $r_\pi = \beta/g_m$ — almost everything else follows.

items.front: Write the transconductance \(g_m\) of a BJT and its room-temperature value at \(I_C = 1\) mA.
items.back: g_m = I_C / V_T, with V_T = kT/q \approx 25-26 mV at 300 K. At I_C = 1 mA, g_m \approx 1 mA / 0.025 V = 40 mS (i.e. 40 mA/V). g_m scales linearly with bias current.
items.hint: Collector current divided by the thermal voltage.

items.front: Define \(r_\pi\) and \(r_o\) in the hybrid-pi model.
items.back: r_\pi = \beta / g_m = \beta V_T / I_C is the small-signal base-emitter input resistance. r_o = V_A / I_C is the output resistance from the Early effect (finite slope of the output curves). The dependent source is g_m*v_be.
items.hint: Input resistance is beta over g_m; output resistance is V_A over I_C.

items.front: What is \(r_e\) (the T-model emitter resistance) and how does it relate to \(g_m\) and \(r_\pi\)?
items.back: r_e = V_T / I_E \approx 1/g_m (since I_E \approx I_C). It is the small-signal resistance looking into the emitter. r_\pi = (\beta + 1) r_e \approx \beta r_e. So r_e is roughly the inverse of transconductance.
items.hint: About 1/g_m; the base sees beta+1 times more.

items.front: What is the intrinsic (maximum) voltage gain of a single BJT, and what sets it?
items.back: The intrinsic gain is g_m*r_o = (I_C/V_T)(V_A/I_C) = V_A / V_T, independent of bias current. With V_A ~ 50-100 V and V_T ~ 25 mV, that is ~2000-4000. Real stage gain |g_m*(R_C||r_o||R_L)| is much lower because the external load dominates.
items.hint: g_m times r_o collapses to Early voltage over thermal voltage.

items.prompt: A BJT biased at I_C = 2 mA with \beta = 150. Using V_T = 25 mV, find g_m and r_\pi.
items.explanation: g_m = I_C/V_T = 2 mA / 25 mV = 80 mS. r_\pi = \beta/g_m = 150/0.080 = 1875 ohm = 1.875 kohm. (Equivalently r_e = V_T/I_E \approx 12.5 ohm and r_\pi = (\beta+1)*r_e.)

items.prompt: A CE stage has g_m = 40 mS and R_C = 5 kohm (r_o and R_L negligible loading). The midband voltage gain A_v is approximately:
items.explanation: A_v = -g_m*R_C = -(0.040 S)(5000 ohm) = -200. The minus sign denotes the 180-degree inversion characteristic of the common-emitter stage.

items.prompt: An unbypassed emitter resistor R_E = 100 ohm is added to a stage with r_e = 12.5 ohm and R_C = 4.7 kohm. The gain magnitude becomes about:
items.explanation: With degeneration, A_v \approx -R_C/(r_e + R_E) = -4700/(12.5 + 100) = -4700/112.5 \approx -42. The R_E swamps r_e, trading gain for linearity and gain stability (the gain now depends mostly on resistors, not g_m).

items.prompt: Why does swamping (unbypassed R_E) make the stage gain more predictable?
choices: Gain becomes \(\approx R_C/R_E\), set by resistor ratio rather than the bias-dependent r_e
It removes the Early effect entirely
items.explanation: When R_E >> r_e, A_v \approx -R_C/R_E. r_e = V_T/I_E varies with bias and temperature, but the resistor ratio is fixed and tolerance-bounded, so the gain is stable and more linear (local series feedback).

items.front: Which capacitances does the high-frequency hybrid-pi add, and which one causes the Miller effect?
items.back: C_\pi (base-emitter diffusion + junction capacitance) and C_\mu (base-collector junction capacitance). C_\mu bridges input and output, so in an inverting CE stage it is multiplied by (1 + |A_v|) at the input (Miller effect), dominating the input pole and limiting bandwidth.
items.hint: Two junction caps; the base-collector one bridges in to out.

lessons.title: CE / CC / CB Topologies, Miller, and the Differential Pair
lessons.content:
The three single-transistor configurations are named by the terminal common to both input and output:

- **Common-emitter (CE):** input at base, output at collector. High voltage gain $-g_m R_C$, inverting; moderate $Z_{in} = r_\pi$ (or $\beta R_E$ with degeneration); moderate $Z_{out} \approx R_C$. The general-purpose gain stage, but bandwidth-limited by Miller.
- **Common-collector (CC) / emitter follower:** input at base, output at emitter. Voltage gain $\approx +1$ (slightly less), high $Z_{in} \approx \beta(r_e + R_E)$, low $Z_{out} \approx r_e + R_{source}/\beta$. A buffer: it gives current gain and isolates a sensitive source from a heavy load without disturbing it.
- **Common-base (CB):** input at emitter, output at collector. Low $Z_{in} \approx r_e$, high $Z_{out}$, non-inverting voltage gain $\approx g_m R_C$. No Miller multiplication (input and output are isolated by the base), so it has excellent high-frequency response — the reason it appears at RF and in the **cascode**.

**Miller effect:** in the CE stage the feedback capacitance $C_\mu$ appears at the input as $C_{\mu}(1 + |A_v|)$. With a gain of, say, 100, a 3 pF $C_\mu$ looks like ~300 pF, forming a low-frequency input pole that sets the bandwidth. The **cascode** (CE driving CB) defeats this: the CB stage holds the CE collector nearly constant, so the CE gain seen by $C_\mu$ is ~$-1$ and Miller multiplication nearly vanishes — high gain *and* wide bandwidth.

**Differential pair:** two matched transistors sharing an emitter tail current source. It amplifies the difference $v_1 - v_2$ (differential gain $\approx g_m R_C$) while rejecting the common-mode component (high CMRR set by the tail source's output resistance). This is the input stage of essentially every op-amp and comparator — the bridge into Module 8.

items.front: Summarize the common-emitter (CE) stage: gain, Z_in, Z_out, phase.
items.back: Voltage gain A_v = -g_m*R_C (high), inverting (180 deg). Z_in = r_\pi (moderate), or \approx \beta*R_E with unbypassed degeneration. Z_out \approx R_C (moderate). Workhorse gain stage; bandwidth limited by the Miller-multiplied C_\mu.
items.hint: High inverting gain, moderate impedances, Miller-limited.

items.front: Summarize the emitter follower (common-collector): gain, Z_in, Z_out, use.
items.back: Voltage gain \approx +1 (non-inverting, slightly less than unity), but high current gain. Z_in \approx \beta(r_e + R_E) (high), Z_out \approx r_e + R_source/\beta (low). Used as a buffer: drives a heavy/low-Z load from a high-Z source without loading it.
items.hint: Unity gain, high input Z, low output Z = buffer.

items.front: Summarize the common-base (CB) stage and why it shines at high frequency.
items.back: Input at emitter (low Z_in \approx r_e), output at collector (high Z_out), non-inverting gain \approx g_m*R_C. Because the base grounds the input from the output, C_\mu sees no Miller multiplication, so CB has wide bandwidth - used at RF and as the top device in a cascode.
items.hint: Low input Z, no Miller, wideband; the cascode's upper transistor.

items.front: State the Miller effect and its quantitative impact on a CE stage.
items.back: A feedback capacitance C_\mu between input and an inverting output of gain -A_v appears at the input as C_\mu*(1 + A_v). In a CE stage with |A_v| = 100, a 3 pF C_\mu looks like ~303 pF, creating a dominant low-frequency input pole that limits bandwidth.
items.hint: Bridging cap multiplied by (1 + gain) at the input.

items.prompt: You need to drive a 50 ohm load from a high-impedance sensor with minimal loading and roughly unity voltage gain. Which topology?
items.explanation: The emitter follower has high input impedance (does not load the sensor), low output impedance (drives the 50 ohm load), unity voltage gain, and current gain. That is the textbook buffer application.

items.prompt: Why does the cascode (CE driving CB) achieve high gain AND wide bandwidth?
choices: The CB stage holds the CE collector nearly constant, so the CE gain seen by C_\mu is ~-1 and Miller multiplication nearly vanishes
It removes the need for biasing
items.explanation: The low input impedance (~r_e) of the CB stage loads the CE collector, so the CE voltage gain into that node is only about -1. Then C_\mu*(1+1) ~ 2*C_\mu instead of (1+100)*C_\mu. The overall gain is recovered at the high-impedance CB output, giving high gain with a much higher bandwidth.

items.prompt: In a BJT differential pair with a tail current source, what primarily sets the common-mode rejection ratio (CMRR)?
choices: The output resistance of the tail current source (higher = better CMRR)
The value of the coupling capacitor
items.explanation: Common-mode signals move both emitters together; the tail source resists that current change. A higher tail output resistance (ideal current source) gives lower common-mode gain and thus higher CMRR. Differential signals split into the tail node, which stays a virtual AC ground, giving full differential gain ~ g_m*R_C.

items.prompt: Which configuration is non-inverting with a voltage gain greater than 1 (much more than unity)?
items.explanation: Common-base gives a non-inverting voltage gain of ~+g_m*R_C (can be large). Common-emitter is inverting (gain negative). Common-collector is non-inverting but only ~+1. So CB is the non-inverting, high-gain option.

items.front: Why is the differential pair the foundation of op-amps and comparators?
items.back: It amplifies the difference of two inputs (A_d \approx g_m*R_C) while rejecting their common-mode average (high CMRR via the tail source). That gives the two matched inputs, high differential gain, DC coupling, and offset performance an op-amp needs - the differential pair is literally the op-amp input stage (Module 8).
items.hint: Amplifies the difference, rejects the common part; matched inputs.

lessons.title: Structure, Regions, and the Square-Law
lessons.content:
A MOSFET is a **voltage-controlled** device: the gate sits on a thin oxide over the channel and steers current with an electric field, drawing essentially **no DC gate current**. The four terminals are gate (G), drain (D), source (S), and body/bulk (B). In an *enhancement* N-channel device, no channel exists until $V_{GS}$ exceeds the threshold $V_{th}$; a *depletion* device conducts at $V_{GS}=0$ and must be pinched off.

With $V_{GS}>V_{th}$, the device operates in one of two regions. For small $V_{DS}$ (specifically $V_{DS}<V_{GS}-V_{th}$) it is in the **triode/linear** region and behaves like a voltage-controlled resistor:

$$I_D = k\left[(V_{GS}-V_{th})V_{DS}-\tfrac{1}{2}V_{DS}^2\right]$$

For $V_{DS}\ge V_{GS}-V_{th}$ the channel pinches off near the drain and current **saturates**, following the classic square-law:

$$I_D = \tfrac{1}{2}k\,(V_{GS}-V_{th})^2(1+\lambda V_{DS})$$

where $k = \mu C_{ox}\,(W/L)$ is the transconductance parameter and $\lambda$ models channel-length modulation (finite output resistance $r_o\approx 1/(\lambda I_D)$).

The quantity $V_{GS}-V_{th}$ is the **overdrive** $V_{ov}$. Coming from a radar/RF background, note the *modern caveat*: in deep-submicron and high-field devices, **velocity saturation** flattens the square-law toward a roughly linear $I_D$ vs $V_{ov}$, so textbook square-law is a teaching idealization, not silicon truth.

items.front: What physically controls drain current in a MOSFET, and what is the DC gate current?
items.back: An electric field from the gate (through the oxide) controls the channel charge. Because the gate is insulated by oxide, the DC gate current is essentially zero, making it a voltage-controlled device.
items.hint: Think 'insulated gate' and 'field effect'.

items.front: Define the enhancement vs. depletion MOSFET in terms of the channel at V_GS = 0.
items.back: Enhancement: no channel at V_GS=0; you must raise V_GS past V_th to create (enhance) one. Depletion: a channel already exists at V_GS=0 and is turned off by depleting it.
items.hint: Does a channel exist with zero gate voltage?

items.front: Write the saturation-region drain current including channel-length modulation.
items.back: $I_D=\tfrac{1}{2}k(V_{GS}-V_{th})^2(1+\lambda V_{DS})$, with $k=\mu C_{ox}(W/L)$. The $(1+\lambda V_{DS})$ term gives a finite output resistance $r_o\approx 1/(\lambda I_D)$.
items.hint: Square-law plus a small slope term.

items.front: What condition (in terms of V_DS and overdrive) separates triode from saturation?
items.back: Saturation when $V_{DS}\ge V_{GS}-V_{th}=V_{ov}$ (channel pinched off, current ~flat). Triode when $V_{DS}<V_{ov}$ (acts like a voltage-controlled resistor).
items.hint: Compare V_DS to the overdrive voltage.

items.front: What is the overdrive voltage V_ov and why does it matter?
items.back: $V_{ov}=V_{GS}-V_{th}$. It sets how hard the device is on. Larger V_ov gives more current and higher g_m but reduces available output swing; designers pick it as a key bias knob.
items.hint: It's the amount V_GS exceeds threshold.

items.prompt: An N-MOSFET has V_th = 1 V, k = 2 mA/V^2, V_GS = 3 V, and is in saturation (ignore lambda). What is I_D?
For small V_DS with V_GS > V_th, the MOSFET behaves most like:
items.explanation: In the triode region near the origin, I_D is roughly proportional to V_DS with a slope set by V_GS, i.e., a voltage-controlled resistance. This is the basis of the MOSFET-as-switch R_DS(on).

items.prompt: Why does the ideal square-law model overpredict current in modern short-channel MOSFETs?
choices: Carrier velocity saturates in the high lateral field, linearizing I_D vs V_ov
items.explanation: In short channels the lateral field is large enough that carrier velocity saturates; I_D then grows more nearly linearly with overdrive rather than as its square, so the textbook square-law is an idealization.

items.prompt: Channel-length modulation (the lambda term) primarily affects which device parameter?
items.explanation: The (1+lambda*V_DS) factor gives a slight upward slope of I_D vs V_DS in saturation, i.e., a finite output resistance r_o ≈ 1/(lambda*I_D), analogous to the BJT Early effect.

items.front: N-MOSFET output (I_D vs V_DS) characteristics
items.back: Each curve is a fixed V_GS. Near the origin (triode) I_D rises with V_DS like a resistor; past the dashed pinch-off parabola (V_DS = V_ov) it flattens into saturation. Higher V_GS gives a higher saturation current, spaced by the square-law.

lessons.title: Biasing and the Small-Signal Model
lessons.content:
To use a MOSFET as a linear amplifier you must **bias** it into saturation at a stable quiescent point $(I_D, V_{DS})$. Because the gate draws no DC current, biasing differs from the BJT: gate resistors set a *voltage*, not a base current.

The most robust scheme is the **voltage-divider bias with a source resistor** $R_S$. The divider sets the gate voltage $V_G$; then $V_{GS}=V_G-I_D R_S$. Source degeneration provides negative feedback: if $I_D$ tries to rise, $I_D R_S$ rises, $V_{GS}$ falls, and $I_D$ is pulled back. This swamps the wide part-to-part spread in $V_{th}$ and $k$. For depletion devices or JFETs, **self-bias** (gate returned to ground through a large resistor, source lifted by $R_S$) works because the device conducts at $V_{GS}=0$.

The key small-signal parameter is the **transconductance**:

$$g_m = \frac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{GS}} = k\,(V_{GS}-V_{th}) = \sqrt{2k\,I_D} = \frac{2 I_D}{V_{ov}}$$

Note $g_m$ rises only as $\sqrt{I_D}$ — weaker than the BJT's $g_m=I_C/V_T$, a real tradeoff. The small-signal model is a gate that draws no current, a current source $g_m v_{gs}$ from drain to source, and an output resistance $r_o=1/(\lambda I_D)$.

The **body effect** matters when source and body differ in potential: it raises $V_{th}$ and adds a back-gate transconductance $g_{mb}=\chi g_m$. Watch for it in stacked or follower stages where the source is not at ground.

items.front: Why can't you bias a MOSFET the way you bias a BJT with a base current?
items.back: The insulated gate draws no DC current, so there is no 'gate current' analogous to base current. You bias by setting a gate voltage (a divider) and let the device's V_GS-I_D relation establish the operating point.
items.hint: Gate current is essentially zero.

items.front: How does a source resistor R_S stabilize the bias point?
items.back: It introduces negative feedback: if I_D rises, the drop I_D*R_S rises, which lowers V_GS = V_G - I_D*R_S and pushes I_D back down. This desensitizes the Q-point to spread in V_th and k.
items.hint: Think series/source degeneration feedback.

items.front: Give three equivalent expressions for MOSFET transconductance g_m in saturation.
items.back: $g_m=k(V_{GS}-V_{th})=\sqrt{2k\,I_D}=\dfrac{2I_D}{V_{ov}}$. All assume square-law saturation operation.
items.hint: Differentiate the square-law I_D with respect to V_GS.

items.front: How does MOSFET g_m scale with bias current compared to a BJT?
items.back: MOSFET: g_m ∝ sqrt(I_D) (since g_m = sqrt(2*k*I_D)). BJT: g_m = I_C/V_T, linear in current. So for the same current a BJT usually has much higher g_m; FETs trade transconductance for high input impedance.
items.hint: One goes as a square root, one is linear.

items.front: What is the body effect and when does it bite?
items.back: When the source-to-body voltage is nonzero, the effective threshold V_th rises and a back-gate transconductance g_mb = chi*g_m appears. It matters in source followers and stacked/cascode devices where the source is not at the body potential.
items.hint: Source not tied to bulk.

items.prompt: A MOSFET is biased at I_D = 2 mA with V_ov = 0.5 V. What is g_m?
items.explanation: $g_m=2I_D/V_{ov}=2(2\text{ mA})/0.5\text{ V}=8\text{ mS}$.

items.prompt: In a voltage-divider + source-resistor bias, V_GS is given by:
items.explanation: The divider sets the gate at V_G (no gate current to load it). The source sits at I_D*R_S above ground, so V_GS = V_G - I_D*R_S, which is the feedback that stabilizes I_D.

items.prompt: Which biasing approach naturally suits a JFET or depletion-mode device?
choices: Self-bias: gate to ground via large R_G, source lifted by R_S
items.explanation: Depletion/JFET devices conduct at V_GS = 0, so self-bias works: the gate is held near ground (no DC gate current) while R_S raises the source, producing a negative V_GS that sets I_D.

items.prompt: Doubling the bias current I_D changes g_m of a square-law MOSFET by a factor of about:
items.explanation: Since g_m = sqrt(2*k*I_D), g_m scales as the square root of I_D; doubling I_D multiplies g_m by sqrt(2) ≈ 1.41.

lessons.title: CS, CD, CG Topologies and High-Z Inputs
lessons.content:
The three single-transistor amplifier topologies mirror the BJT trio. **Common-source (CS)** is the workhorse (≈ common-emitter): input at gate, output at drain. Its mid-band gain is

$$A_v = -g_m(R_D\,\|\,r_o)$$

giving high voltage gain with a $180^\circ$ inversion. Adding an *unbypassed* source resistor degenerates it: $A_v\approx -g_m R_D/(1+g_m R_S)$, trading gain for linearity and bandwidth. Input impedance is essentially **infinite** at DC (gate is insulated); output impedance is $\approx R_D\,\|\,r_o$.

**Common-drain / source follower (CD)** (≈ emitter follower): output at source. Gain is just under unity,

$$A_v = \frac{g_m R_S}{1+g_m R_S}\approx 1$$

with very high input and **low output impedance** $\approx 1/g_m$ — a buffer. Watch the body effect, which slightly lowers gain.

**Common-gate (CG)** (≈ common-base): input at source, gate grounded. Non-inverting, gain $A_v\approx g_m R_D$, **low input impedance** $\approx 1/g_m$, good high-frequency behavior (no Miller multiplication of $C_{gd}$). It pairs with CS to form the **cascode**.

The defining FET advantage is the **enormous gate input impedance** (gigaohms to teraohms). This is what makes JFET/MOSFET front-ends ideal for **electrometers, pH probes, photodiode/charge amplifiers, and other high-impedance sensors** where any input bias current would corrupt the measurement. JFETs in particular offer very low noise at high source impedance. The price you pay is lower $g_m$ per unit current than a BJT.

items.front: State the mid-band voltage gain of a common-source amplifier (source fully bypassed).
items.back: $A_v=-g_m(R_D\,\|\,r_o)$. The minus sign is the 180-degree phase inversion; it is the FET analog of the BJT common-emitter stage.
items.hint: Transconductance times the load it drives, inverted.

items.front: What does an unbypassed source resistor R_S do to a CS amplifier?
items.back: It degenerates (negative feedback): gain drops to about -g_m*R_D/(1+g_m*R_S), but linearity, bandwidth, and Q-point stability improve, and output swing distortion falls.
items.hint: Trade gain for linearity/bandwidth.

items.front: Give the gain and output impedance of a source follower (common-drain).
items.back: Gain $A_v=g_mR_S/(1+g_mR_S)\approx 1$ (slightly less). Output impedance $\approx 1/g_m$ (low). High input impedance. It is a buffer/level shifter.
items.hint: Just under unity gain, low Z_out.

items.front: What are the input impedance and frequency advantage of a common-gate stage?
items.back: Low input impedance ≈ 1/g_m, non-inverting gain ≈ g_m*R_D. Its frequency response is good because there is no Miller multiplication of C_gd, so it is used as the top device in a cascode.
items.hint: Input at source, gate grounded, no Miller effect.

items.front: Why are FET front-ends preferred for high-impedance sensors like electrometers and pH probes?
items.back: The insulated/reverse-biased gate has essentially no input bias current and gigaohm-plus input impedance, so it does not load or corrupt high-impedance sources. JFETs add very low noise at high source impedance.
items.hint: Think about what input bias current does to a teraohm source.

items.prompt: Which MOSFET topology provides voltage gain near unity but low output impedance (a buffer)?
items.explanation: The common-drain / source follower has gain just under 1 and output impedance ≈ 1/g_m, making it the FET buffer, analogous to the BJT emitter follower.

items.prompt: A CS amplifier has g_m = 5 mS and R_D || r_o = 4 kohm (source fully bypassed). The voltage gain is about:
items.explanation: $A_v=-g_m(R_D\|r_o)=-(5\text{ mS})(4\text{ k}\Omega)=-20$.

items.prompt: The cascode (CS + CG) is favored at high frequency mainly because the common-gate device:
choices: Suppresses Miller multiplication of C_gd, raising bandwidth
items.explanation: By holding the CS drain at low impedance, the CG device kills the voltage swing across C_gd that causes Miller multiplication, so the high-frequency pole moves up and bandwidth improves while gain stays high.

items.prompt: Compared to a BJT at the same bias current, a typical small MOSFET has:
choices: Higher g_m and lower input impedance
Lower g_m but far higher input impedance
Higher g_m and higher input impedance
items.explanation: FET g_m scales as sqrt(I_D) and is generally lower than a BJT's I_C/V_T at the same current, but the insulated gate gives orders-of-magnitude higher input impedance — the core BJT-vs-MOSFET tradeoff.

items.front: Common-source amplifier schematic
items.back: Input couples to the gate (high Z); the drain resistor R_D converts the g_m*v_gs current into an inverted output voltage v_out = -g_m*(R_D||r_o)*v_in. The source returns to ground (bypassed for full gain). This is the FET workhorse gain stage.

lessons.title: MOSFET as a Switch and the CMOS Inverter
lessons.content:
Drive the gate hard ($V_{GS}\gg V_{th}$) and the MOSFET collapses into deep triode, becoming a small resistor: the **on-resistance** $R_{DS(on)}$. Conduction loss is then $P_{cond}=I_D^2 R_{DS(on)}$, so a low $R_{DS(on)}$ power MOSFET wastes little power when fully on — the basis of every switching regulator (Module 13) and motor/LED driver.

Switching is not free, because the gate is a **capacitor**. The relevant figure of merit is the **total gate charge** $Q_g$ (with components $Q_{gs}$, $Q_{gd}$). Drive current and transition speed are linked by $Q_g = I_{gate}\,t$. During each transition both voltage across and current through the device are nonzero, producing **switching loss**:

$$P_{sw} \approx \tfrac{1}{2}\,V_{DS}\,I_D\,(t_{on}+t_{off})\,f_{sw}$$

which grows linearly with switching frequency. The flat **Miller plateau** in $V_{GS}$ during turn-on (charging $Q_{gd}$) dominates the transition; this is why a stiff, low-impedance **gate driver** matters. Power MOSFETs also contain an intrinsic **body diode** (the source-body junction) that freewheels inductive current — useful in synchronous bridges but slow, so external Schottky diodes are often added.

At the digital frontier sits the **CMOS inverter**: a PMOS pull-up over an NMOS pull-down, gates tied together. For a low input the PMOS is on (output high); for a high input the NMOS is on (output low). In either static state one device is off, so **static current is nearly zero** — power is consumed only during transitions ($P\approx C V_{DD}^2 f$) plus brief shoot-through. This is the literal analog-to-digital seam (→ Module 03) and the reason CMOS scaled to billions of gates.

items.front: What is R_DS(on) and what loss does it produce in a switch?
items.back: The drain-source on-resistance when the gate is driven hard and the device is in deep triode. It causes conduction loss P_cond = I_D^2 * R_DS(on). Low R_DS(on) power MOSFETs minimize on-state heating.
items.hint: Resistor behavior of a fully-on switch.

items.front: Why does the gate need a substantial drive current to switch fast, even though DC gate current is zero?
items.back: The gate is a capacitor with total gate charge Q_g. Switching means moving that charge: Q_g = I_gate * t. To switch in a short time you must supply a large transient current, which is why low-impedance gate drivers are used.
items.hint: Q = I*t for the gate capacitance.

items.front: Write an estimate of MOSFET switching loss and state how it scales with frequency.
items.back: $P_{sw}\approx\tfrac{1}{2}V_{DS}I_D(t_{on}+t_{off})f_{sw}$. It scales linearly with switching frequency, so faster switching trades lower component size for higher switching loss.
items.hint: Overlap of V and I during transitions, times f.

items.front: What is the Miller plateau in the V_GS turn-on waveform?
items.back: A flat region in V_GS where gate drive current is diverted into charging C_gd (Q_gd) while the drain voltage swings. The transition speed is gated here, so it dominates switching time and motivates a strong gate driver.
items.hint: Flat spot in gate voltage while V_DS moves.

items.front: What is the body diode of a power MOSFET and what is it good (and bad) for?
items.back: The intrinsic source-body PN junction. It freewheels inductive current in synchronous/bridge circuits (good), but it is slow with poor reverse recovery (bad), so external Schottky diodes are often added in parallel.
items.hint: Source-to-drain junction inherent to the structure.

items.front: Why does a static CMOS inverter draw almost no DC power?
items.back: In either logic state one of the complementary devices (PMOS pull-up or NMOS pull-down) is off, breaking the path from V_DD to ground. Power is consumed mainly during switching: P ≈ C*V_DD^2*f, plus brief shoot-through.
items.hint: One transistor is always off in a static state.

items.prompt: A power MOSFET conducts 10 A with R_DS(on) = 20 mohm. The conduction loss is:
items.explanation: $P_{cond}=I^2R_{DS(on)}=(10\text{ A})^2(0.020\,\Omega)=2\text{ W}$.

items.prompt: Switching loss in a hard-switched MOSFET increases when you:
choices: Raise the switching frequency or slow the transitions
Use a stiffer gate driver to switch faster
items.explanation: P_sw scales with f_sw and with the V-I overlap time (t_on + t_off). Higher frequency or slower edges increase loss; a stiffer driver that shortens transitions reduces it.

items.prompt: In a CMOS inverter, when the input is LOW (near 0 V), the output is:
choices: LOW, because the NMOS is on
HIGH, because the PMOS is on and NMOS is off
items.explanation: A low input turns the PMOS pull-up on and the NMOS pull-down off, pulling the output to V_DD (HIGH). The complementary action gives the inversion with near-zero static current.

items.prompt: When switching an inductive load (e.g., a motor) with a low-side MOSFET, you should:
choices: Rely only on the slow body diode for the flyback current
Add a freewheeling diode (or use synchronous rectification) to clamp the inductive flyback
Increase R_DS(on) to absorb the energy
items.explanation: Interrupting inductor current produces a large flyback voltage. A freewheeling/clamp diode (or synchronous high-side device) gives the current a path and protects the MOSFET; the intrinsic body diode alone is slow and lossy.

lessons.title: The Ideal Op-Amp and the Golden Rules
lessons.content:
An **operational amplifier** is a high-gain differential amplifier: it outputs $V_{out}=A_{ol}(V_+ - V_-)$, where the **open-loop gain** $A_{ol}$ is enormous (typically $10^5$ to $10^7$). The *ideal* op-amp idealizes this into four assumptions: **infinite open-loop gain**, **infinite input impedance** (no current flows into the inputs), **zero output impedance**, and **infinite bandwidth**.

The magic happens with **negative feedback** — routing some of the output back to the inverting ($V_-$) input. Combined with infinite gain, this forces the two **golden rules**:

1. **No voltage difference:** $V_+ = V_-$. For a finite output the differential input must be $V_{out}/A_{ol}\to 0$. The feedback drives the inverting input to track the non-inverting input. When $V_+$ is grounded, $V_-$ becomes a **virtual ground**.
2. **No input current:** $I_+ = I_- = 0$, from infinite input impedance.

These two rules let you derive every standard configuration by inspection — apply KCL at the inputs and assume $V_+=V_-$. The op-amp is not an amplifier you set the gain *of*; it is a near-perfect *error nuller* that does whatever its output must do to keep $V_+ - V_- = 0$. The external resistor network sets the closed-loop gain, not the silicon. This is why op-amp circuits are so reproducible: gain depends on resistor *ratios*, which are easy to match, not on transistor parameters that drift.

Think of it like a servo loop from your radar days: the output slews until the feedback signal balances the command input.

items.front: Write the defining input-output relation of an op-amp and name the four ideal assumptions.
items.back: Output: $V_{out}=A_{ol}(V_+ - V_-)$. Ideal assumptions: (1) infinite open-loop gain $A_{ol}\to\infty$, (2) infinite input impedance ($I_+=I_-=0$), (3) zero output impedance, (4) infinite bandwidth.
items.hint: It amplifies the DIFFERENCE; idealize gain, both impedances, and bandwidth.

items.front: State the two golden rules of the ideal op-amp with negative feedback.
items.back: Rule 1: $V_+ = V_-$ (the inputs are at the same voltage; the op-amp drives its output to make this true). Rule 2: $I_+ = I_- = 0$ (no current enters either input). Both require negative feedback to hold.
items.hint: Equal input voltages; zero input current.

items.front: Why does infinite open-loop gain force $V_+ = V_-$ under negative feedback?
items.back: Since $V_{out}=A_{ol}(V_+ - V_-)$, the differential input is $V_+ - V_- = V_{out}/A_{ol}$. For any finite, bounded output, as $A_{ol}\to\infty$ the difference $\to 0$. Negative feedback supplies the corrective path that actually drives it there.
items.hint: Solve for the input difference; divide a finite output by infinity.

items.front: What is a 'virtual ground' and when does it appear?
items.back: When $V_+$ is tied to ground (0 V) and negative feedback holds, golden rule 1 forces $V_-=0$ V as well, even though no wire connects it to ground. The inverting node sits AT ground potential but sinks/sources no current into ground. It appears in the inverting amplifier, summing amp, integrator, and transimpedance amp.
items.hint: Inverting input held at 0 V by feedback, with no real connection.

items.prompt: An op-amp has $A_{ol}=10^6$ and its output sits at $5\,\text{V}$. What is the differential input voltage $V_+ - V_-$?
items.explanation: $V_+ - V_- = V_{out}/A_{ol} = 5/10^6 = 5\times10^{-6}\,\text{V} = 5\,\mu\text{V}$. It is tiny but not exactly zero; the 'virtual short' is an approximation that gets better as gain rises.

items.prompt: Why is gain set by external resistor RATIOS rather than the op-amp's internal gain?
choices: Negative feedback makes closed-loop gain depend on the feedback network, and resistor ratios match/track far better than transistor parameters
The op-amp has no internal gain at all
Internal gain is exactly 1 by design
items.explanation: With large loop gain, $A_{cl}$ depends on the feedback ratio, not $A_{ol}$. Matched resistor ratios are reproducible and temperature-tracking, so the circuit is predictable despite huge part-to-part variation in raw transistor gain.

items.prompt: Golden rule 2 (no input current) is a direct consequence of which ideal assumption?
items.explanation: Infinite input impedance means $I=V/Z\to 0$ at each input. Infinite gain gives rule 1 ($V_+=V_-$); infinite input impedance gives rule 2 ($I_+=I_-=0$).

items.prompt: Why must NEGATIVE feedback be present for the golden rules to apply?
choices: Negative feedback creates a self-correcting loop that drives the differential input toward zero; with positive feedback the output runs to a rail instead
Negative feedback increases the open-loop gain
Positive feedback also satisfies the golden rules equally well
The rules hold open-loop with no feedback at all
items.explanation: Negative feedback subtracts a sample of the output, nulling the error so $V_+\approx V_-$. Positive feedback reinforces the error, saturating the output at a supply rail (the basis of comparators and Schmitt triggers), so the golden rules do NOT apply there.

items.front: Conceptually, how is an op-amp in a feedback loop like a servo?
items.back: It is an error nuller: it slews its output to whatever value makes the feedback signal equal the command (so $V_+ - V_- \to 0$). Like a position servo driving until feedback matches the setpoint, the op-amp does 'whatever it takes' at its output to balance its inputs.
items.hint: Output moves until the feedback cancels the input error.

lessons.title: Core Configurations: Inverting, Non-Inverting, Buffer, Summing, Difference
lessons.content:
Apply the golden rules and KCL to derive the workhorse circuits.

**Inverting amplifier.** $V_+$ grounded, so $V_-$ is a virtual ground. Input current through $R_{in}$ equals feedback current through $R_f$ (rule 2): $\frac{V_{in}-0}{R_{in}} = \frac{0-V_{out}}{R_f}$, giving
$$A_v = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = -\frac{R_f}{R_{in}}.$$
Input impedance is just $R_{in}$ (modest), and the gain is negative (180-degree phase inversion).

**Non-inverting amplifier.** $V_{in}$ drives $V_+$; a feedback divider from $V_{out}$ sets $V_-$. Rule 1 gives $V_{in}=V_-=V_{out}\frac{R_g}{R_g+R_f}$, so
$$A_v = 1+\frac{R_f}{R_g}.$$
Gain is always $\ge 1$, positive, with near-infinite input impedance.

**Voltage follower (buffer).** Non-inverting with $R_f=0$, $R_g=\infty$: $A_v=1$. Huge input Z, tiny output Z — an impedance converter that stops a load from disturbing a source.

**Summing amplifier.** Several inputs into the virtual-ground node through their own resistors: $V_{out}=-R_f\left(\frac{V_1}{R_1}+\frac{V_2}{R_2}+\dots\right)$. Inputs don't interact because the node is a virtual ground (the analog-mixer and binary-weighted-DAC trick).

**Difference amplifier.** Resistors on both inputs subtract: with matched ratios, $V_{out}=\frac{R_f}{R_{in}}(V_2-V_1)$. Its common-mode rejection depends entirely on resistor matching — the weakness the instrumentation amp later fixes.

items.front: Derive and state the gain of the inverting amplifier.
items.back: Virtual ground at $V_-$: KCL gives $\frac{V_{in}}{R_{in}}=-\frac{V_{out}}{R_f}$, so $A_v=-\frac{R_f}{R_{in}}$. Input impedance $=R_{in}$; output is inverted (180 degrees).
items.hint: Current in equals current out at the virtual ground.

items.front: State the gain of the non-inverting amplifier and its key trait.
items.back: $A_v = 1+\frac{R_f}{R_g}$. Always $\ge 1$, non-inverting, with very high input impedance (signal drives the high-Z $V_+$ directly). Cannot attenuate below unity.
items.hint: One plus the feedback ratio; minimum gain is 1.

items.front: What is a voltage follower and what problem does it solve?
items.back: A non-inverting amp with $A_v=1$ (output tied directly to $V_-$). It provides very high input impedance and very low output impedance, isolating a high-impedance source from a heavy load so the load can't pull the source down. Pure impedance buffering / fan-out.
items.hint: Unity gain; impedance converter / buffer.

items.front: Why don't the inputs of a summing amplifier interact with each other?
items.back: Each input resistor terminates at the virtual-ground node, held at 0 V by feedback. Because that node never moves, the current from one input is unaffected by the others: $V_{out}=-R_f\sum_i \frac{V_i}{R_i}$. The virtual ground decouples the channels (used in audio mixers and binary-weighted DACs).
items.hint: All currents land on a fixed 0 V node, so they add independently.

items.prompt: An inverting amp has $R_f = 100\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $R_{in} = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$. With $V_{in} = +0.2\,\text{V}$, $V_{out}$ is:
items.explanation: $A_v=-R_f/R_{in}=-100\text{k}/10\text{k}=-10$. $V_{out}=-10\times 0.2=-2\,\text{V}$. The minus sign reflects the 180-degree inversion of the inverting topology.

items.prompt: A non-inverting amp needs a gain of exactly 11. A valid resistor pair is:
items.explanation: $A_v=1+R_f/R_g=1+100\text{k}/10\text{k}=1+10=11$. The pair 10k/100k gives $1+0.1=1.1$; equal resistors give 2; $R_f=0$ gives a follower (gain 1).

items.prompt: A difference amplifier's common-mode rejection in practice is limited mainly by:
choices: Resistor ratio mismatch between the two input branches
items.explanation: The subtraction $V_{out}=\frac{R_f}{R_{in}}(V_2-V_1)$ relies on the two $R_f/R_{in}$ ratios being identical. Any mismatch lets common-mode signal leak through, degrading CMRR. This is precisely why the three-op-amp instrumentation amp exists.

items.prompt: A summing amp has $R_f=10\,\text{k}\Omega$, with $V_1=1\,\text{V}$ through $R_1=10\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $V_2=2\,\text{V}$ through $R_2=5\,\text{k}\Omega$. $V_{out}$ is:

items.front: Inverting amplifier schematic
items.back: Inverting amplifier: input resistor Rin to the virtual-ground inverting node, feedback resistor Rf from output back to that node, non-inverting input grounded; gain Av = -Rf/Rin.

items.front: Compare input impedance of the inverting vs non-inverting amplifier.
items.back: Inverting: input impedance equals $R_{in}$ (the signal drives a resistor into a virtual ground), which is finite/modest. Non-inverting: input impedance is the op-amp's own input impedance (near-infinite), because the signal drives the high-Z $V_+$ pin directly. Choose non-inverting (or a buffer) for high-impedance sources.
items.hint: Inverting = Rin; non-inverting = very high.

lessons.title: Integrators, Differentiators, and Transimpedance Amps
lessons.content:
Replace feedback resistors with reactive elements and the op-amp performs calculus on the signal.

**Integrator.** Inverting topology with a capacitor $C$ as the feedback element and $R$ at the input. The virtual-ground current $V_{in}/R$ charges $C$, so
$$V_{out}(t) = -\frac{1}{RC}\int V_{in}\,dt.$$
In the frequency domain $H(j\omega)=-\frac{1}{j\omega RC}$ — gain falls at $-20$ dB/decade (a low-pass / ramp response). Pure integrators drift: DC offset and bias current charge $C$ until the output rails, so a large $R$ across $C$ is added to bound the DC gain.

**Differentiator.** Swap them — $C$ at the input, $R$ in feedback: $V_{out}=-RC\frac{dV_{in}}{dt}$, gain rising at $+20$ dB/decade. It amplifies high-frequency noise and is prone to instability, so practical versions add a series input resistor to tame the high-frequency gain.

**Transimpedance amplifier (TIA).** A current-to-voltage converter: the input current flows into the virtual ground and through feedback resistor $R_f$, giving
$$V_{out} = -I_{in}R_f.$$
This is *the* photodiode front-end. The diode's current drives the virtual ground (so the diode sees about 0 V bias, minimizing dark current and leakage nonlinearity), and $R_f$ sets the gain in volts-per-amp. A small feedback capacitor $C_f$ across $R_f$ is almost always needed for stability, because the photodiode's junction capacitance forms a noise-gain peak that can make the TIA oscillate.

items.front: Write the time-domain and frequency-domain response of an ideal op-amp integrator.
items.back: Time: $V_{out}(t)=-\frac{1}{RC}\int V_{in}\,dt$. Frequency: $H(j\omega)=-\frac{1}{j\omega RC}$, magnitude falling at $-20$ dB/decade. $R$ at the input, $C$ in feedback.
items.hint: C in feedback; output is the negative integral scaled by 1/RC.

items.front: Why does a practical integrator need a resistor in parallel with the feedback capacitor?
items.back: An ideal integrator has infinite DC gain, so any input offset voltage or bias current is integrated and charges $C$ until the output saturates at a rail. A large parallel resistor $R_f$ sets a finite DC gain ($-R_f/R$) and a low-frequency corner, bounding the drift while still integrating above that corner.
items.hint: Infinite DC gain integrates offsets to a rail; the resistor bounds DC gain.

items.front: What is a transimpedance amplifier and what is its gain expression?
items.back: A current-to-voltage converter: input current flows into the virtual ground and through feedback resistor $R_f$, giving $V_{out}=-I_{in}R_f$. Gain (transimpedance) is $R_f$ in volts-per-amp. It is the standard photodiode and current-output-sensor front-end.
items.hint: Current in, voltage out; gain = Rf in V/A.

items.front: Why is a TIA preferred over a simple load resistor for reading a photodiode?
items.back: The virtual ground holds the photodiode at essentially 0 V (or a fixed bias), so its junction capacitance doesn't slow the response with signal swing and dark-current nonlinearity is minimized. A plain load resistor lets the diode voltage swing, adding an RC pole and bias-dependent error. The TIA gives high gain with wide bandwidth and a defined diode bias.
items.hint: Holds the diode at constant (0 V) bias; no signal-dependent voltage swing.

items.prompt: An integrator has $R=10\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $C=0.1\,\mu\text{F}$. A constant $V_{in}=+1\,\text{V}$ is applied. The output ramps at:
items.explanation: $\frac{dV_{out}}{dt}=-\frac{V_{in}}{RC}=-\frac{1}{10^4\cdot10^{-7}}=-\frac{1}{10^{-3}}=-1000\,\text{V/s}$. A positive constant input produces a negative-going ramp (until it rails).

items.prompt: A photodiode delivers $2\,\mu\text{A}$ into a TIA with $R_f=1\,\text{M}\Omega$. The output voltage magnitude is:
items.explanation: $|V_{out}|=I_{in}R_f=2\times10^{-6}\times10^{6}=2\,\text{V}$. The 1-megohm feedback resistor converts microamps to volts at 1 V per microamp.

items.prompt: Why is the pure op-amp differentiator (C in, R feedback) problematic in practice?
choices: Its gain rises at +20 dB/decade, amplifying high-frequency noise and threatening stability
It has infinite DC gain and rails on offsets
It cannot pass any AC signal
items.explanation: Differentiator gain $\propto j\omega RC$ climbs with frequency (+20 dB/dec), boosting wideband noise and creating a phase-margin problem that can oscillate. A series input resistor (and/or feedback cap) limits the high-frequency gain to fix it.

items.prompt: Why does a TIA usually need a small feedback capacitor $C_f$ across $R_f$?
choices: The photodiode capacitance plus Rf forms a noise-gain peak that can make the TIA unstable; Cf adds a compensating pole to restore phase margin
Cf increases the DC transimpedance gain
Cf converts the TIA into an integrator
Cf is needed to bias the photodiode
items.explanation: The diode's junction capacitance $C_d$ and $R_f$ create a noise-gain zero that peaks the loop response and erodes phase margin (ringing/oscillation). A feedback $C_f$ places a pole that flattens the noise gain and stabilizes the amplifier, trading some bandwidth for stability.

items.front: Gain-bandwidth tradeoff (GBW)
items.back: Open-loop gain rolls off at -20 dB/decade to the unity-gain frequency fT; a higher closed-loop gain has a lower shelf and smaller bandwidth, illustrating that closed-loop gain times bandwidth equals the constant gain-bandwidth product.

lessons.title: Non-Idealities: GBW, Slew Rate, Offset, CMRR, PSRR
lessons.content:
Real op-amps depart from ideal in ways that decide whether a design works.

**Finite gain-bandwidth product (GBW).** Open-loop gain is large only at DC and rolls off at $-20$ dB/decade (internally compensated for stability). The product of closed-loop gain and bandwidth is roughly constant and equals the **unity-gain frequency** $f_T$:
$$\text{GBW} = A_{cl}\cdot f_{-3\text{dB}} \approx f_T.$$
A part with $\text{GBW}=10\,\text{MHz}$ run at a gain of 100 gives only about 100 kHz of bandwidth. This is a *small-signal* limit.

**Slew rate (SR).** The maximum rate the output can change, in V/us — a *large-signal* limit set by how fast the internal compensation cap can charge: $\text{SR}=I_{max}/C_c$. A fast, big step gets a triangular (slew-limited) edge, and the **full-power bandwidth** is $f_{max}=\text{SR}/(2\pi V_{peak})$. GBW and slew are independent: an amp can have wide small-signal bandwidth yet slew-limit on large signals.

**Input offset voltage ($V_{os}$).** A small (uV to mV) DC error that the op-amp behaves as if applied between its inputs, from transistor mismatch. It is amplified by the noise gain, producing a DC output error — critical in high-gain or precision DC paths. **Bias and offset currents** ($I_B$, $I_{os}$) flow into the inputs and create errors across source/feedback resistances.

**CMRR** quantifies rejection of signals common to both inputs ($\text{CMRR}=20\log_{10}(A_d/A_{cm})$); **PSRR** quantifies rejection of supply-rail variation. Both are large at DC and degrade with frequency.

items.front: Define gain-bandwidth product and the gain-bandwidth tradeoff.
items.back: $\text{GBW}=A_{cl}\cdot f_{-3\text{dB}}\approx f_T$ (the unity-gain frequency). Because open-loop gain rolls off at $-20$ dB/decade, raising closed-loop gain lowers closed-loop bandwidth proportionally: their product is constant. It is a small-signal limit.
items.hint: Gain times bandwidth equals a constant (the unity-gain frequency).

items.front: Define slew rate and contrast it with GBW.
items.back: Slew rate is the maximum output rate of change, in V/us, $\text{SR}=I_{max}/C_c$ — a LARGE-signal limit (the output literally can't move faster). GBW is a SMALL-signal (linear) bandwidth limit. They are independent: a part can have ample GBW yet slew-limit on a large fast step, producing triangular edges.
items.hint: SR = large-signal V/us limit; GBW = small-signal bandwidth; independent.

items.front: What is input offset voltage $V_{os}$ and how does it appear at the output?
items.back: A small DC voltage (uV to mV) the op-amp behaves as if it were applied between its inputs, caused by internal transistor mismatch. It is multiplied by the circuit's noise gain to give a DC output error. It dominates precision DC measurements and high-gain stages; zero-drift/chopper amps minimize it.
items.hint: Equivalent DC error at the input, amplified by the noise gain.

items.front: Define CMRR and PSRR.
items.back: CMRR (common-mode rejection ratio) = ratio of differential gain to common-mode gain, $\text{CMRR}=20\log_{10}(A_d/A_{cm})$ dB — how well the amp ignores a signal present equally on both inputs. PSRR (power-supply rejection ratio) = how well it ignores variation on its supply rails. Both are high at DC and fall with frequency.
items.hint: CMRR rejects common-mode input; PSRR rejects supply ripple.

items.prompt: An op-amp with $\text{GBW}=10\,\text{MHz}$ is used at a closed-loop gain of 100. Its closed-loop $-3\,\text{dB}$ bandwidth is about:
items.explanation: $f_{-3\text{dB}}=\text{GBW}/A_{cl}=10\,\text{MHz}/100=100\,\text{kHz}$. Raising gain shrinks bandwidth proportionally because their product is fixed.

items.prompt: An op-amp has $\text{SR}=1\,\text{V}/\mu\text{s}$. The full-power bandwidth for a $10\,\text{V}$ peak sine is approximately ($f_{max}=\text{SR}/(2\pi V_{pk})$):
items.explanation: $f_{max}=\frac{\text{SR}}{2\pi V_{pk}}=\frac{1\times10^{6}\,\text{V/s}}{2\pi\cdot10}=\frac{10^{6}}{62.8}\approx 1.59\times10^{4}\,\text{Hz}\approx16\,\text{kHz}$. Above this, the large 10 V sine slew-limits into a triangle.

items.prompt: A precision amp has $V_{os}=50\,\mu\text{V}$ in a stage with a noise gain of 1000. The DC output error from offset is:
items.explanation: Output offset error $=V_{os}\times\text{noise gain}=50\,\mu\text{V}\times1000=50\,\text{mV}$. In high-gain DC paths even microvolt-level offsets become significant; chopper/zero-drift parts cut $V_{os}$ to the low microvolts.

items.prompt: You apply a fast 5 V step to an op-amp circuit and see the output rise as a straight-line ramp rather than tracking immediately. This indicates:
items.explanation: A constant-slope (triangular/ramp) edge is the signature of slew-rate limiting: the output is moving at its maximum V/us and cannot follow the commanded step. Small-signal bandwidth (GBW) would instead show an exponential-looking settling, not a straight ramp.

items.front: Why do bias and offset currents matter, and how do you minimize their error?
items.back: Input bias currents $I_B$ flow through the source and feedback resistances, creating offset voltages ($I_B\times R$). Matching the DC resistance seen by both inputs makes the equal $I_B$ terms cancel, leaving only the smaller offset current $I_{os}$. Use low resistor values, a bias-current-compensation resistor, or FET-input op-amps (pA-level $I_B$).
items.hint: I_B times resistance = error; match input resistances or use FET inputs.

items.front: Why does CMRR (and PSRR) degrade at high frequency, and why does it matter?
items.back: Rejection comes from internal balance/feedback that weakens as open-loop gain rolls off with frequency, so high-frequency common-mode or supply noise leaks to the output. It matters because real signals ride on common-mode noise and supply ripple; a spec quoted at DC overstates rejection of fast interference (e.g., switcher noise on the rails).
items.hint: Rejection tracks the falling loop gain; HF noise leaks through.

lessons.title: Comparators, Schmitt Triggers, and the Instrumentation Amp
lessons.content:
Two specialized uses round out op-amp fluency.

**Comparators (open-loop, positive feedback).** Without feedback, the huge gain slams the output to a rail whenever $V_+ \ne V_-$ — a 1-bit analog-to-digital decision. But a plain op-amp is a *poor* comparator: it isn't optimized for fast saturation/recovery, and near the threshold input noise causes the output to **chatter** (multiple false transitions). Dedicated comparator ICs have fast outputs and often open-collector stages.

**Schmitt trigger (hysteresis).** Add *positive* feedback so the threshold depends on the output state: the upper trip point $V_{TH}$ and lower trip point $V_{TL}$ differ, creating a **hysteresis window** $V_H = V_{TH}-V_{TL}$. Once it switches, the input must travel all the way to the *other* threshold to switch back, so noise smaller than $V_H$ can't cause chatter. Essential for clean edges from noisy or slow signals.

**Instrumentation amplifier.** The difference amp's CMRR is hostage to resistor matching and it loads the source. The **three-op-amp instrumentation amp** fixes both: two non-inverting input buffers (high input impedance, no source loading) provide differential gain set by a *single* gain resistor $R_G$, feeding a matched difference amp that does the subtraction and rejects common mode. Overall:
$$A_v = \left(1+\frac{2R_1}{R_G}\right)\frac{R_3}{R_2}.$$
It delivers very high CMRR with one-resistor gain control — the standard front-end for bridges, thermocouples, and small differential sensor signals on a large common-mode background.

items.front: How does an op-amp behave as an open-loop comparator?
items.back: With no feedback, the enormous open-loop gain saturates the output to the positive rail when $V_+>V_-$ and the negative rail when $V_+<V_-$. It makes a 1-bit decision: which input is larger. The output is essentially digital (rail-to-rail).
items.hint: Huge gain, no feedback => output pinned to whichever rail the sign demands.

items.front: Why is an ordinary op-amp a poor comparator?
items.back: It isn't designed for fast saturation and recovery (slow, may exhibit phase reversal), and—lacking hysteresis—near the threshold input noise drives the output to chatter (many spurious transitions). Dedicated comparator ICs have fast outputs, defined logic levels, and often open-collector/open-drain stages.
items.hint: Slow recovery, possible phase reversal, and noise chatter at threshold.

items.front: What is a Schmitt trigger and what does its hysteresis window accomplish?
items.back: A comparator with positive feedback so the trip point depends on the current output state: upper threshold $V_{TH}$ and lower threshold $V_{TL}$ differ, giving a hysteresis window $V_H=V_{TH}-V_{TL}$. Once switched, the input must cross the OTHER threshold to switch back, so noise smaller than $V_H$ cannot cause chatter. It yields clean edges from noisy/slow inputs.
items.hint: Positive feedback => two thresholds => noise immunity, no chatter.

items.front: Why does the three-op-amp instrumentation amp beat a plain difference amp?
items.back: Two non-inverting buffers give very high input impedance (no source loading) and provide differential gain via a single resistor $R_G$, while the matched output difference amp rejects common mode. Result: very high CMRR, high input Z, and one-resistor gain control. The plain difference amp loads the source and has CMRR limited by resistor matching.
items.hint: Buffered high-Z inputs + one gain resistor + matched diff stage = high CMRR.

items.prompt: A noisy DC signal slowly crosses a comparator's threshold and the output produces a rapid burst of high/low transitions. The fix is:
choices: Add positive feedback to create a Schmitt trigger with a hysteresis window larger than the noise
Add a feedback capacitor to integrate the input
Use negative feedback to set a precise gain
items.explanation: The chatter comes from noise repeatedly crossing a single threshold. Hysteresis (positive feedback) separates the up/down thresholds by more than the noise amplitude, so once it switches it stays switched until a genuine, larger excursion occurs.

items.prompt: An instrumentation amp has $R_1=25\,\text{k}\Omega$ (each), a unity-gain difference stage ($R_3/R_2=1$), and $R_G=1\,\text{k}\Omega$. Its gain $A_v=1+2R_1/R_G$ is:
items.explanation: $A_v=1+\frac{2R_1}{R_G}=1+\frac{2\times25\text{k}}{1\text{k}}=1+50=51$ (with the difference stage at unity). One resistor $R_G$ sets the whole gain.

items.prompt: The primary reason an instrumentation amp is chosen to read a strain-gauge bridge is:
choices: It extracts a tiny differential signal riding on a large common-mode voltage with very high CMRR and without loading the bridge
It has the highest slew rate available
It provides the lowest possible output impedance
It inverts the signal for digital logic
items.explanation: Bridge outputs are small differential signals sitting on a substantial common-mode level. The in-amp's high input impedance avoids unbalancing the bridge, and its high CMRR rejects the common-mode component while amplifying only the difference.

items.prompt: A Schmitt trigger has $V_{TH}=+2\,\text{V}$ and $V_{TL}=-1\,\text{V}$. Its hysteresis window is:
items.explanation: $V_H=V_{TH}-V_{TL}=2-(-1)=3\,\text{V}$. Any input noise with amplitude under 3 V (within the window) cannot cause a false re-trigger once the output has switched.

items.front: Name modern op-amp/in-amp variants and what problem each solves.
items.back: Zero-drift/chopper/auto-zero: near-zero offset and 1/f noise for precision DC. Rail-to-rail input/output: maximizes swing on low single-supply rails. Low-noise/precision: best $V_{os}$ and noise density for instrumentation. FET/CMOS-input: pA bias currents for high-impedance sources. Single-supply parts simplify systems lacking a negative rail (use a virtual-ground reference).
items.hint: Chopper=low offset; rail-to-rail=swing; FET=low bias; single-supply=virtual ground.

items.front: In a single-supply design, what is a 'virtual ground' reference and why use it?
